Main Characteristics: The CTA is the most important actionable element, tied to the primary goal (sales/signups). It must capture immediate attention via visual weight.
Do's: Use a unique color or style that doesn't compete with other screen elements. Ensure enough contrast with the background. Use wording that calls to an action.
Don'ts: Do not use more than one instance of a primary CTA on one page to avoid dividing user attention. Do not use ghost buttons for main CTAs.

Main Characteristics: Visual hierarchy directs the user to what matters most. De-emphasizing secondary information makes the interface less noisy.
Do's: Use font weight and color instead of just size to control hierarchy; de-emphasize tertiary content using light grey.
Don'ts: Don't let every element compete for attention; don't use font weights under 400 for small UI text.

Main Characteristics: Use of the design is easy to understand, regardless of the user's experience, knowledge, language skills, or current concentration level.
Do's: Eliminate unnecessary complexity; be consistent with user expectations and intuition.
Don'ts: Not in source

Main Characteristics: Users have a pre-existing representation in their heads of how a product or system works based on past experience.
Do's: Do research to understand the mental models of your target audience. Design the conceptual model to match the mental model.
Don'ts: Don't let the conceptual model 'bubble up' from the technology/database structure.

Main Characteristics: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
Do's: Provide the same means of use for all users: identical whenever possible; equivalent when not.
Don'ts: Avoid segregating or stigmatizing any users.

Main Characteristics: Users use visual design (look and feel) as the first indicator of whether to trust a site.
Do's: Use professional layout, color, and font to pass the initial 'trust rejection' phase. Ensure content is credible for long-term trust.
Don'ts: Don't neglect first impressions; users will reject a site based on poor design before even reading the content.

Main Characteristics: Design modules in the master Sketch file must have an exact equivalent in the code.
Do's: Ensure names in the Sketch file and the code match exactly.
Don'ts: Let art direction and engineering get out of sync.

Main Characteristics: Research-driven design to ensure the right product is built for the user.
Do's: Conduct research, analyze data, and talk to users before making design assumptions.
Don'ts: Make assumptions about user requirements without grounded data.

Main Characteristics: The highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
Do's: Release updates and new versions frequently to meet changing needs. Focus on high-value activities.
Don'ts: Wait for long release cycles (e.g., 18-24 months) or until the end of a cycle to deliver functionality.

Main Characteristics: The team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts behavior.
Do's: Conduct regular retrospectives to identify and learn from what is or isn't working.
Don'ts: Ignore team feedback or shut down conversation during review sessions.

Main Characteristics: Lack of data and data structure severely impedes the user's ability to find relevant products and their desire to purchase.
Do's: Develop several automated and manual data-structure verification processes to catch common product data issues.
Don'ts: Do not leave product specs unstructured (lumped together in a single column) or allow missing/redundant tags.

Principle: Which Attributes to Include in the List Item
Main Characteristics: Without sufficient and relevant list item information, users are likely to discard perfectly relevant products.
Do's: Include price, thumbnail, title/type, variations, and user ratings plus 1-3 category-specific attributes.
Don'ts: Don't use a 'one size fits all' approach; tailor attributes to the vertical.

Main Characteristics: Users often reject categories if the first products don't represent the breadth of the scope.
Do's: Ensure the first 10-20 products reflect major product types within the category.
Don'ts: Don't use 'Price: Low to High' as the default sorting.

Main Characteristics: Users cannot find, narrow down, and verify compatibility-dependent products without specific compatibility filters.
Do's: Offer filters for the specific attribute that determines fit (e.g., laptop size, camera mount).
Don'ts: Don't rely on 'Brand' as the only compatibility filter.

Main Characteristics: Users are sent on product page detours when product unavailability isn't clearly indicated.
Do's: Clearly mark items as 'sold out', 'pre-order', or 'unavailable for home delivery' in the list item.
Don'ts: Don't hide availability status until the product page is reached.

Principle: Feature Broad Range of Types on Homepage
Main Characteristics: First-time users rely on homepage visuals to infer the site's product catalog and breadth. Narrow selections lead to misinterpretation.
Do's: Ensure a broad selection of different product types are featured; use images or thumbnails; dedicate space to non-popular categories.
Don'ts: Only feature a small selection of product types; assume users will know the site sells other items.

Principle: Filters vs. Categories for Shared Attributes
Main Characteristics: Wrongly implementing filters as categories prevents users from mixing criteria (e.g., 'mummy' + 'down' sleeping bag).
Do's: Use categories for mutually exclusive hierarchy scopes; use filters for attributes where multiple can be selected.
Don'ts: Implement product type as a category if attributes are the same across types; use brand-based categories.

Main Characteristics: Redundancy makes it difficult to know which option to select and makes the site look unprofessional.
Do's: Manually create the taxonomy; map manufacturer data to your own curated categories; check for combined categories.
Don'ts: Use auto-generated categorization; reuse manufacturer categories without merging across vendors.

Main Characteristics: Users struggle to find and verify compatibility (e.g. laptop chargers). Completion rates are very low without explicit cross-linking.
Do's: Create unique pages for each compatibility product; include series-level pages; link these in cross-sell sections.
Don'ts: Assume users know their model's specific specs; force users to manually compare dimensions from different pages.

Principle: Avoid Relying Solely on Thematic Categories
Main Characteristics: Users expect a site to be structured by 'type of product.' Purely thematic navigation breaks mental models.
Do's: Use thematic paths as secondary options; ensure the core navigation is based on product types.
Don'ts: Replace product type categories with brand or occasion categories entirely.

Principle: Main Nav Categories as First Hierarchy Level
Main Characteristics: Hiding categories behind a singular "Products" link causes double-hover issues and prevents inferring site type at a glance.
Do's: Place top-level categories permanently in the main navigation bar; use non-product categories (e.g. "Deals") alongside them.
Don'ts: Scope the entire catalog within a solitary "Products" item; use double-hover activation areas with short heights.

Main Characteristics: Non-product links (Contact, Sign-in, Shipping) clutter the header but are vital for those who need them.
Do's: Use white space or background colors to separate courtesy links; group them into sections; include a 'Shipping' link.
Don'ts: Place courtesy navigation below primary product navigation; omit vital policy links from the header entirely.

Principle: Have First 1-2 Levels as Sub-Category Pages
Main Characteristics: Sub-category pages act as storefronts, clearing up ambiguity via thumbnails and descriptions before showing products.
Do's: Display sub-categories high on the page; include thumbnails; provide a "View All" option for each scope.
Don'ts: Display a product list for broad categories; push sub-category links below the fold with featured products.

Principle: Avoid Dividing or Sub-Dividing Product Lists
Main Characteristics: Thematic divisions make lists hard to sort and can lead users to think they've reached the end of results prematurely.
Do's: Use product type filters instead of sectional dividers; show a single continuous list of products.
Don'ts: Divide lists into thematic sections on one page; use product sliders or tabs to sub-divide featured results.

Main Characteristics: To minimize deviations, modules are specified precisely in master files.
Do's: Define typography treatments, use an 8-pixel grid for spacing, and document interactions precisely.
Don'ts: Allow inconsistent naming or deviations from the master Sketch file across platforms.

Main Characteristics: Familiarity with shapes associated with action improves recognition speed. Deviation increases identification time.
Do's: Use rectangles or rounded rectangles as the safest choice. Ensure buttons stand out from other elements.
Don'ts: Do not use organic or irregular shapes (triangle, circle) unless the style requires it.

Main Characteristics: Physical size affects the ease of tapping or clicking. Too small targets lead to frustration and errors.
Do's: Start with 44 x 44 points for mobile (50 points ideal). For cursor-based devices, 32 x 32 points is acceptable.
Don'ts: Do not make buttons smaller than the recommended minimum.

Main Characteristics: Contrast ratio determines readability and accessibility for users, including those with impaired vision.
Do's: Aim for at least AA contrast ratio between labels and backgrounds. Use saturation that works in light/dark modes.
Don'ts: Do not use low contrast ratios that make buttons look like decoration; avoid high contrast that causes eye strain.

Main Characteristics: Clarity of the primary message or action takes precedence over aesthetics.
Do's: Identify the single most important thing you want users to see and do first.
Don'ts: Fragment the user experience with conflicting messages or multiple equal-weight calls to action.

Main Characteristics: Designing for exploration and error prevention.
Do's: Design interactions so they are impossible to mis-tap; treat UI like a children's toy.
Don'ts: Create fragile interfaces where users can easily make irreversible errors.

Main Characteristics: Prioritizing long-term utility and accessibility over temporary design trends.
Do's: Adopt technology only when it serves a proven purpose for the majority of users.
Don'ts: Introduce stylistic features like parallax effects just for the sake of following a trend.

Main Characteristics: Every element must have a purpose contributing to the larger goal.
Do's: Remove any element if its purpose cannot be explicitly explained.
Don'ts: Add decorative elements that do not contribute to functionality or core brand message.

Main Characteristics: Distinguishing between navigation and data manipulation.
Do's: Use buttons for changing/manipulating data; use links for navigating between pages.
Don'ts: Use primary button styles for simple navigation tasks that don't involve state changes.

Main Characteristics: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
Do's: Evaluate progress by the incorporation of design into functional code; demonstrate user stories.
Don'ts: Judge progress solely by the number of design artifacts produced or documentation.

Main Characteristics: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility.
Do's: Continually refine design decisions throughout the cycle.
Don'ts: Treat design as a one-time activity at the kickoff or milestone.

Main Characteristics: The art of maximizing the amount of work not done is essential. Focus on essential user needs.
Do's: Focus only on what is most necessary; seek the simplest way to write code.
Don'ts: Spend time on wireframes for implemented designs; squeeze in features without evaluating impact.

Main Characteristics: Regularly integrating code to reveal problems quickly and ensure a working solution.
Do's: Rebuild and retest code several times a day; automate unit tests.
Don'ts: Wait for a traditional test cycle at the end of development.

Main Characteristics: Kanban principle to reduce average lead time and improve quality.
Do's: Address blocking issues quickly; reduce queue and buffer sizes.
Don'ts: Take on more tasks than the team's capacity allows simultaneously.

Main Characteristics: Agile modeling value where representatives provide info and make decisions in real-time.
Do's: Involve stakeholders daily; invite them to iteration reviews to provide feedback.
Don'ts: Exclude the organization or work in a vacuum within a single delivery team.

Main Characteristics: The brain can only process a small amount of information at a time. Providing only what is needed avoids overwhelming users.
Do's: Show people what they need when they need it. Reveal details only as needed contextually.
Don'ts: Don't give too much information all at once. Don't worry about counting clicks if they reduce cognitive load.

Main Characteristics: Different demands use different mental resources. Cognitive load is the most expensive.
Do's: Minimize cognitive load even if it increases motor load (clicks). Ensure targets are large enough (Fitt's Law).
Don'ts: Don't make people think or remember more than necessary. Don't make targets too small or far apart.

Main Characteristics: Time is relative; mental processing and expectations influence frustration.
Do's: Always provide progress indicators (skeleton screens, average-color blocks). Keep task time consistent.
Don'ts: Don't make users wait without feedback; don't use empty white screens or center-page spinners only.

Main Characteristics: Providing instant responses to user actions even while background validations occur to reduce perceived latency.
Do's: Register an action instantly in the UI while syncing in the background.
Don'ts: Do not show a loading spinner for every minor interaction; don't make users wait for complex checks.

Main Characteristics: Motion fits naturally into the experience with intention to help users reach goals.
Do's: Use animation to surface connections between states or provide feedback.
Don'ts: Do not use motion just for the sake of moving; avoid 'jokey' indicators for critical services.

Main Characteristics: Optimal timing changes based on viewport size and element complexity.
Do's: Use 100-200ms for small components; 500-700ms for page transitions.
Don'ts: Don't make users 'watch the clock' by using slow progress indicators or spinners.

Principle: Start with a feature, not a layout
Main Characteristics: The shell of the app is secondary to functionality. Designing features first provides navigation context.
Do's: Start with a piece of actual functionality, like a search field or a specific button.
Don'ts: Don't start by designing the navigation bar or the page container.

Main Characteristics: Abstractly figuring out every edge case is inefficient. It is easier to solve problems in a usable interface.
Do's: Work in short cycles: design a simple version, build it, and iterate on the working design.
Don'ts: Don't try to design every single feature and every edge case before moving to implementation.

Main Characteristics: Having too many options causes decision fatigue. Constraints facilitate consistency.
Do's: Define a restrictive type scale and a limited color palette (8-10 shades) in advance.
Don'ts: Don't pick values like font sizes or hex codes one pixel or shade at a time from a limitless pool.

Principle: Text needs consistent contrast on images
Main Characteristics: Background images are dynamic and can make text unreadable.
Do's: Add a semi-transparent overlay; lower image contrast; or add a soft text shadow to increase legibility.
Don'ts: Don't place text directly on a busy image without a contrast-improving treatment.

Main Characteristics: Design decisions should be based on target audience demographics and physical context.
Do's: Account for viewing distance, indoor/outdoor use, and input methods; adjust text size and contrast.
Don'ts: Don't ignore the suitability of the interface style for age groups; don't apply mobile variables to TV.

Main Characteristics: Inclusive design benefiting all users, including those with impairments.
Do's: Use minimum 12pt font (16pt preferred); build accessibility into the process from the start.
Don'ts: Don't rely on one sense alone; don't use serif type or italics for large blocks; avoid 'Click here'.

Main Characteristics: Maintaining predictable behavior and layout across multiple screens.
Do's: Keep margins and placement of common items (title bars, back buttons) the same; use grids.
Don'ts: Don't allow objects to 'jump around' between different views of the interface.

Main Characteristics: Sizing interactive elements for finger/thumb operation.
Do's: Use a minimum touch area of 7mm x 7mm; leave at least a 2mm gap between items.
Don'ts: Don't place touch targets too close together, leading to accidental presses.

Main Characteristics: Designing to minimize user mistakes through constraints, feedback, and safe arrangement.
Do's: Use warnings and confirmations; provide default values; allow undos.
Don'ts: Don't let users cruise through hazardous procedures on 'autopilot'.

Main Characteristics: Making the overall experience feel like a coherent service across multiple devices.
Do's: Specify which functionality belongs on each device; create guidelines that span multiple device types.
Don'ts: Don't treat devices as isolated cells; avoid disjointed UIs.

Main Characteristics: Interfaces depend on visual representation of objects and rapid, reversible operations.
Do's: Ensure interface actions are aligned with user understanding; provide immediate feedback.
Don'ts: Don't distance the user from the consequences of their actions.

Main Characteristics: Allowing users to experience benefits before requiring investment of effort or data.
Do's: Let users engage with core features before asking them to sign up; prompt for sign-in during task saving.
Don'ts: Don't use 'Sign up' and 'Sign in' together as the terms are confusingly similar.

Main Characteristics: Agile processes harness change for competitive advantage, even late in development.
Do's: Solicit feedback often; embrace additions or subtractions from the prioritized list.
Don'ts: Stick blindly to a roadmap; lock down requirements very early (Waterfall style).

Main Characteristics: Deliver working software frequently, with a preference for shorter timescales.
Do's: Aim for sprints typically between one and four weeks; deliver shippable functionality.
Don'ts: Allow prototypes to remain unavailable; rely on long-release cycles that cause feature lag.

Main Characteristics: Build projects around motivated individuals; give them support and trust.
Do's: Trust the team to make necessary decisions that affect the final output.
Don'ts: Treat staff as interchangeable resources rather than human beings with unique skills.

Main Characteristics: Design communicates necessary info regardless of ambient conditions or sensory abilities.
Do's: Use different modes (pictorial, verbal, tactile) for redundant presentation of essential information.
Don'ts: Not in source

Main Characteristics: The design accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
Do's: Provide choice in methods of use; accommodate right- or left-handed access.
Don'ts: Not in source

Main Characteristics: If users overlook the item count, they lack feedback on the quality of their search or category scope.
Do's: Clearly show the total number of items in the list both above and below the list.
Don'ts: Don't make the count difficult to see or omit it entirely.

Main Characteristics: Users are often unsure about their current scope and position in the site hierarchy.
Do's: Include hierarchy-based breadcrumbs on category pages to help users traverse up.
Don'ts: Don't omit breadcrumbs from the list pages, even if they are present on product pages.

Main Characteristics: Non-standard methods are difficult for users to control and can distract from list items.
Do's: Rely on standard navigation for product lists. Use novel methods only for immersive storytelling.
Don'ts: Don't use parallax scrolling or other non-standard methods for actual product selection.

Main Characteristics: Different contexts require different pagination methods. A hybrid approach is often best.
Do's: Use 'Load More' combined with lazy-loading/Endless Scroll for initial items (~50-100).
Don'ts: Don't use pure endless scrolling if it blocks the footer; avoid pure pagination for categories.

Principle: Default Number of Products Per Page
Main Characteristics: Too few products limit the grasp of breadth; too many encourage aimless glancing.
Do's: Load 50-150 items for categories, 25-75 for search, and 15-30 for mobile.
Don'ts: Don't load a very small number of items by default (e.g., 10-18) unless on mobile.

Main Characteristics: Users have a difficult time evaluating value for products sold in different package sizes.
Do's: Calculate and display price per unit (e.g., $/oz) and allow users to sort by it.
Don'ts: Don't force users to do their own math to find value for money.

Principle: Include Rating Average and Number of Ratings
Main Characteristics: Users find rating averages suspicious unless they can see the volume of data.
Do's: Show the star average and the count of ratings in parentheses (e.g., 4.5 stars (118)).
Don'ts: Don't show stars without the review count.

Main Characteristics: Users prefer products with many good ratings over a single perfect rating.
Do's: Take the number of ratings into account in the sorting algorithm.
Don'ts: Don't use strict arithmetic that places one 5-star review above a 4.8-star review with hundreds of votes.

Principle: Avoid Ads in Prime Homepage Locations
Main Characteristics: Users perceive internal promotions looking like ads as "spam," setting a negative tone.
Do's: Be mindful of size, placement, and aesthetics; integrate promotions into the overall design.
Don'ts: Place ads in the middle column or upper part of page; use pop-up banners or dialog overlays.

Main Characteristics: Auto-rotation helps exposure but can cause frustration if too fast or changing during click.
Do's: Use 5-10s rotation; pause on hover; permanently stop after user interaction.
Don'ts: Rotate too quickly; make vital features accessible ONLY via a carousel slide.

Principle: Assist Selection of Scope from Homepage
Main Characteristics: Success rates linked to how quickly users find a specific scope. Broad detours lead to false stock conclusions.
Do's: List popular categories/sub-categories on homepage; promote wizards; use visual hierarchy for paths.
Don'ts: Force users to meticulously plow through the entire taxonomy tree from the root.

Principle: Invest in Bespoke Imagery and Design
Main Characteristics: Snap decisions about brand quality stick. Positive impressions increase patience with hiccups.
Do's: Use large, high-quality images of products in use context; curate design; invest in photography.
Don'ts: Rely solely on single products on white backgrounds; use cluttered or "old-fashioned" layouts.

Main Characteristics: Tailoring increases relevance but can interfere with the homepage's role as an 'escape route'.
Do's: Explicitly label personalized sections; keep personalization moderate; maintain static sections.
Don'ts: Personalize core features like navigation; make changes so dramatic the site becomes unrecognizable.

Principle: Be Cautious with Default Site Scopes
Main Characteristics: Users don't expect to be in a sub-section (like 'Women') upon landing on the homepage.
Do's: Only use if one audience massively outweighs others; make the current scope abundantly obvious.
Don'ts: Assume users will easily find how to switch scopes; use if the audience is diverse.

Main Characteristics: Users feel overwhelmed when presented with too many choices.
Do's: Aim for ~10 or fewer sub-categories per group; use nesting; prioritize by importance.
Don'ts: Allow every department to add options to the top level; provide long, flat lists of 20+ items.

Main Characteristics: Users expect grouping headers in menus to be clickable. Non-clickable headers block browsing.
Do's: Ensure headers/groups are selectable and part of the hierarchy; use pointer cursor on hover.
Don'ts: Use shallow text labels for grouping; force users into narrow sub-scopes because parent is unclickable.

Main Characteristics: Users look for accessories (e.g. laptop bags) within the theme of the main product.
Do's: Place an "Accessories" sub-category within relevant high-level categories; keep consistent.
Don'ts: Pollute main list with accessories (e.g. 100 pillows in Sofa); use global 'Accessories' as only path.
